name: The Keres (Violent Death Spirits)
shortDescription: The Keres were the spirits of violent death in Greek mythology, drawn to battlefields and scenes of slaughter. They represented different forms of violent demise, contrasting with the peaceful death embodied by Thanatos.
description: The Keres (Κῆρες) were female spirits of violent death in Greek mythology. Unlike Thanatos, the personification of peaceful death, the Keres represented gruesome and unavoidable ends, such as death in battle, accidents, or disease. They were often depicted as winged figures, sometimes monstrous, with a thirst for blood, swooping down upon battlefields to claim the souls of the dying. They were the daughters of Nyx (Night), born without a father, and were associated with the darker aspects of fate and mortality. Each Keres was drawn to a specific type of violent death, and they would compete for the souls of the slain. Their presence instilled fear and dread, as they were harbingers of suffering and destruction. While generally feared, they were also seen as an inevitable part of the cosmic order, representing the harsh realities of mortality. Although frequently conflated, the Keres are distinct from the Erinyes (Furies), who avenged crimes against kin, and the Moirai (Fates), who determined the course of life. The Keres are more specifically connected to the moment of violent death itself.
